title: Colorimetric LAMP/RT-LAMP Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"b9psl","text":"This protocol describes how to perform colorimetric LAMP / RT-LAMP reactions with homemade buffers, together with home-brewed BstLF and MMLV enzymes. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":39,"length":38}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"632tf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5v3qu","text":"It is based on previous protocols of our lab (see LAMP/RT-LAMP Reaction protocol), but since two of the most common indicators used in colorimetric LAMP (phenol red and neutral red) require weakly buffered reactions, the buffer composition needed to be changed. This was done according to indications by Poole et al (2017).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":153,"length":64}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":50,"length":30},{"key":1,"offset":304,"length":18}],"data":{}},{"key":"fgv33","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1d22q","text":"The colorimetric methods rely on the inherent proton production by DNA polymerases during the amplification process, which leads to a drop in the reaction pH that can be followed using these pH indicators. The results can be seen directly with the \"naked eye\", without the need for equipment or hands-on processing (Tanner et al., 2015).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":316,"length":19}],"data":{}},{"key":"65etm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ervu","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"4qa2i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"385E63A06F2011EDA91DBBA8CE9803CC","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/lamp-rt-lamp-reaction-protocol-eq2lyp8dqlx9/v1"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"6D67A1806F2311EDA91DBBA8CE9803CC","url":"https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0169011"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"56A79EA06F3C11ED8CB80B18E4600303","url":"https://www.future-science.com/doi/full/10.2144/000114253"}},"3":{"type":"image","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"647E961B341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","height":286.9854537825155,"is_video":false,"legend":"{\"blocks\":[{\"key\":\"5ljvn\",\"text\":\"Color change of neutral red and phenol red indicator dyes in LAMP reactions. \",\"type\":\"unstyled\",\"depth\":0,\"inlineStyleRanges\":[{\"style\":\"bold\",\"offset\":0,\"length\":77}],\"entityRanges\":[],\"data\":{}},{\"key\":\"94jcq\",\"text\":\"Before amplification (T0), reactions containing neutral red are colorless. Samples turn pink if positive or a brownish yellow if negative as shown after a sixty-minute (T60) amplification. Reactions containing phenol red are pink at T0 and remain pink if negative but turn yellow if positive as shown here after a sixty-minute (T60) amplification. [Figure from Poole et al (2017)].\",\"type\":\"unstyled\",\"depth\":0,\"inlineStyleRanges\":[{\"style\":\"sub\",\"offset\":23,\"length\":1},{\"style\":\"sub\",\"offset\":170,\"length\":2},{\"style\":\"sub\",\"offset\":234,\"length\":2},{\"style\":\"sub\",\"offset\":329,\"length\":2}],\"entityRanges\":[{\"key\":0,\"offset\":361,\"length\":18}],\"data\":{}}],\"entityMap\":{\"0\":{\"type\":\"link\",\"mutability\":\"MUTABLE\",\"data\":{\"guid\":\"C485A2046F3C11EDB77B0A58A9FEAC02\",\"url\":\"https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0169011\"}}}}","mime":"image/png","original_name":"image.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/files/mkvgbmtz7.jpg","source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/mkvcbmtz7.jpg","type":1,"webp_placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/files/mkvfbmtz7.webp","webp_source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/mkvbbmtz7.webp","width":374.03317436109785}}}}
steps: Prepare 1000 µL of 2X Colorimetric Buffer Mix according to the following table: Two main differences compared to non-colorimetric LAMP reaction are the addition of dNTPs in the buffer, as they influence the reaction pH, and the removal of Tris-HCl as this component is vital to maintain the pH of the buffer at a stable point, which is not desirable for pH-dependent detection The optimal concentration of KCl for home-brewed BstLF is 50 mM (see LAMP/RT-LAMP Buffer protocol )
Prepare the LAMP or RT-LAMP reaction mix On ice according to the following tables:
Measure the reaction pH with strips and adjust pH using NaOH 250 mM to a final value of pH 8-9. The pH of the original solution should be around 5-6 before adjusting.
Add nuclease-free water to a final volume of 1000 µL . Make aliquots and store them at 4 °C until use.

Incubate the reactions at 65ºc for 35 minutes and observe results with the naked eye. These are expected results with the use of neutral red as a colorimetric indicator, with negative reactions (NTC) staying yellow and positive reactions (in this case, six5) turning pink:
Vortex and spin reaction mix. Always keep reactions and components On ice to reduce spurious amplification risk or sample degradation.

Incubate the reaction at 65 °C for 00:35:00 Time can be between 20 - 60 minutes depending on the specific assay. Specific temperatures can vary according to the optimal temperature of the primer set used. The reaction can be carried out in any heater device from a water bath to real-time machines but the performance of the reactions can vary with respect to the temperature control.
Visualize results with the naked eye. With neutral red, you should expect results similar to these:
creator: Felipe Navarro Martínez
